headline: 24-hr road works set in Metro Manila for Holy Week | ABS-CBN News
text:
Members of the Metro Manila Development Authority (MMDA) assist motorists as a carousel bus merges onto private vehicle traffic near the West Avenue area in Quezon City on August 7, 2023. Jonathan Cellona, ABS-CBN News/File
MANILA -- There will be 24-hour road works on major thoroughfares in Metro Manila this Holy Week, according to the Metropolitan Manila Development Authority. 
The MMDA said the Department of Public Works and Highways (DPWH) and a telecommunications firm will take advantage of the summer break, even as a significant number of motorists and travelers head to the provinces for religious observances and family gatherings.
The road works will begin on Holy Wednesday (March 27) at 11 pm and continue round-the-clock until Monday (April 1) at 5 am. 
They will be undertaken at the following areas:
1. Batasan-Commonwealth Tunnel, Filinvest 1 Road to fronting Sandigan (1st lane from plantbox, Quezon City)
2. West Avenue, Ligaya St. to Del Monte Avenue (1st lane from sidewalk), Quezon City
3. Luzon Avenue SB, Congressional Avenue Ext. to before Luzon Flyover (1st lane from center), Quezon City
4. Commonwealth Avenue, Landbank to Elliptical Road (3rd lane from sidewalk), Quezon City
5. Mindanao Avenue, Tandang Sora Avenue to Longines St. (truck lane), Quezon City
6. Mindanao Avenue Tunnel to Sauyo Road (truck lane), Quezon City
7. Payatas Road, Batasan Road to Bayanihan St., Quezon City
8. Payatas Road between Singko St. & Leyte St., Quezon City
9. Payatas Road before Maynilad Pumping Station to Petron Gas Station (1st lane from sidewalk), Quezon City
10. IBP-San Mateo Road, Quezon City
11. Roxas Boulevard NB between Lourdes St. & Vicente Sotto St., Pasay City
12. Roxas Boulevard NB between Remedios St. & Pasaje Del Carmen St., Pasay City
13. Roxas. Boulevard EDSA Flyover SB bridge approach (outer lane & inner lane), Pasay City
14. South Super Highway East Service Road, Parañaque City
15. EDSA NB Guadalupe before bridge (3rd lane), Makati City
16. EDSA NB Guadalupe P. Burgos St. near Jollibee and McDonald's (4th lane), Makati City
17. EDSA NB Guadalupe JP Rizal Ext. (2nd lane), Makati City
18. EDSA NB Guadalupe JP Rizal Ext. near Stoplight (2nd lane), Makati City
Motorists are advised to take alternate routes. The affected roads will be fully passable by 5 a.m. on Monday (April 1).
MMDA Acting Chairman Atty. Don Artes said the Holy Week break is the best time for the contractors to complete their work works and reblocking activities since "fewer vehicles are on Metro Manila streets during that period."
"The clearance for the 24-hour road works aims to strike a balance between maintaining infrastructure and minimizing disruptions and inconvenience to motorists after the significantly religious holidays," he said.

headline: NBI returns to PH without ex-lawmaker Teves | ABS-CBN News
text:
Former Negros Oriental Rep Arnolfo Teves after he was arrested by Timor-Leste authorities. The NBI team was allowed by Timor-Leste President Jose Ramos-Horta to talk to Teves while he is still being processed. Handout/NBI
MANILA – The National Bureau of Investigation (NBI) has returned back to the Philippines but failed to bring home expelled Negros Oriental congressman Arnolfo Teves Jr. from Timor-Leste.
NBI Director Medardo De Lemos on Sunday said that Timor-Leste authorities are still evaluating the request of the International Criminal Police Organization (Interpol) and the country’s request for the custody of the former congressman. 
“Legal process po ito ng Timor (...) at tayo naman bilang requesting party sa pamamagitan ng Interpol ay mag-aantay kung ano ang termination point at process nila doon sa kanilang bansa,” De Lemos said. 
Teves camp pinag-aaralang magreklamo sa UN council 
He also debunked reports that Philippine authorities who tried to capture a photo of Teves inside a detention cell did so in order to mock him, saying that it was only a “proof of life.” 
“Tayo naman ay pinahintulutan ng Presidente mismong ng Timor-Leste na si President Jose Ramos-Horta na kumuha ng litrato parang proof of life na nasa kanila na nga po si Congressman Teves,” he said. 
“Eh sabi namin papaano namin malalaman na si Congressman Teves ‘yan. Hindi nila mai-harap sa amin si Congressman Teves dahil nakadapa ang tao sa selda, kung si Congressman man yun eh naawa kami,” he added. 
According to De Lemos, President Ramos-Horta is also hoping for a faster evaluation so Teves will be sent back immediately where he will be facing trial for being the alleged mastermind in the murder of Negros Oriental governor Roel Degamo and nine others in Pamplona last year. 
Teves' legal team mulls options to prevent deportation 
Meanwhile, De Lemos assured that Teves is in a good condition.
“Ang sabi nga niya, natatakot siya sa kanyang buhay so sabi namin nirerespeto namin po yan at kami po ay may commitment na hindi sya mapapahamak at walang mangyayaring masama sa kanya, tapusin lang ang proseso,” he said. 
Teves was arrested by Timor-Leste’s Scientific and Criminal Investigation Police in Dili on Thursday while playing golf, after being placed in “Red Notice” by Interpol in February.

headline: DOE: Luzon grid might experience 'Yellow Alert' in April, May | ABS-CBN News
text:
People flock to a waterpark at the Pasig Rainforest Park in Pasig City on March 2, 2024 as temperatures rise in the metro with visitors sometimes reaching up to the thousands says park management. Maria Tan, ABS-CBN News
MANILA -- Luzon's power grid may fall under a "Yellow Alert" warning in the coming months due to El Niño's effects on hydroelectric power plants, according to the Department of Energy (DOE).
"Based on the latest DOE simulations, with hydroelectric power plants running below capacity level due to the El Niño phenomenon, the Luzon grid might experience Yellow Alert in April and May while the Visayas and Mindanao grids will have normal reserve level during the second quarter of the year," DOE said in a statement Sunday (March 24, 2024).
With this, the department urged the public to practice energy conservation as the country prepares for the dry season.
DOE added that it continues to monitor the Philippines' power situation to "ensure energy security, especially during the next three months when scorching temperatures are felt."
Meanwhile, the DOE also reminded power generation companies to comply with the DOE-approved operation and maintenance program to ensure meeting target operational dates.
The dry season in the country has begun last Friday (March 22, 2024) and was announced by state weather bureau PAGASA.
READ: Dry season is here, says PAGASA
READ: Hot, humid weather expected during Holy Week

headline: Hot, humid weather expected during Holy Week | ABS-CBN News
text:
Devotees flock to Baclaran Church in Parañaque City on Palm Sunday, March 24, 2024. Mark Demayo, ABS-CBN News
MANILA -- Warm and humid weather due to the Easterlies is expected throughout the country this coming Holy Week, according to the state weather bureau.
PAGASA reported on Sunday, March 24, that Metro Manila will experience sunny and humid conditions with scattered rain showers and thunderstorms from Holy Wednesday through Good Friday.
Hot and humid conditions, especially at midday and early afternoon, with possible scattered thunderstorms in the afternoon or the evening are also expected in the rest of the country.
Thunderstorms are expected in Southern Luzon from Monday to Tuesday; Palawan from Monday to Friday; Central Luzon from Tuesday to Friday; Northern Luzon from Thursday to Saturday; Visayas from Monday to Friday; and Mindanao from Monday to Friday.
Intense rains are expected from ones Tuesday to Thursday over Davao, SOCCSKSARGEN, BARMM, and the Zamboanga Peninsula.
RELATED VIDEO:

headline: Advocates call for zero waste, plastic reduction ahead of Holy Week | ABS-CBN News
text:
Uncollected garbage lay along the side of a street in Brgy. Bagong Lipunan in Quezon City on January 4, 2024. Mark Demayo, ABS-CBN News/File
MANILA -- With the onset of the Holy Week, pro-environment supporters advocating for a “zero waste and toxics-free future” gathered in Manila on Sunday, carrying a cross to represent the detrimental impact of single-use plastics (SUPs) on the environment.
The participants started at Manila High School, traversing Kalye Victoria Street and neighboring barangays before culminating at the Manila Cathedral.
Ochie Tolentino, a zero-waste campaigner from the EcoWaste Coalition, emphasized the symbolic act of bearing the cross to highlight the environmental challenges posed by SUPs.
“Kami po ngayon, symbolically nagkakaroon ng pagpapasan ng krus bilang pagkilala at pagpapahatid na kung gaano ang single-use plastic ay nagpapahirap sa ating kalikasan,” said Tolentino.
Tolentino urged the Church to embody Pope Francis’ "Laudato si," advocating for the stewardship of our planet by minimizing the use of SUPs, especially during the Holy Week.
She also called on the local and national government to enforce existing laws such as the Ecological Solid Waste Management Act, Clean Air Act, and Extended Producer Responsibility measures in communities to facilitate proper waste segregation and reduce plastic consumption.
She also urged the Department of Science and Technology and the Department of Environment and Natural Resources to expedite the release of the list of non-environmentally acceptable products or plastic alternatives, stressing the necessity of the list so companies can adopt eco-friendly packaging.
Tolentino hopes that major industries and companies will transition to the use of non-plastic packaging, highlighting the contribution of residual waste from these sources to the clogging of drainages and floods.
She also encouraged the public to have discipline and initiative in waste management.
“[Panawagan] sa buong mamayan ng Pilipinas na sana magkaroon tayo ng disiplina at pagkakataong tumulong na makiisa sa panawagan ng gobyerno na tayo’y maghiwalay ng basura sa ating mga nabubulok, sa ating mga kalakal, sa ating mga panapon, at sa ating mga delikadong basura dahil sa pamamagitan lang ng paghihiwalay ng basura natin, matutulungan ang ating pamahalaang bayan mula sa national hanggang lokal na mabawasan ang ating mga basura,” she said.

headline: Floating barrier inilatag ng China sa Ayungin Shoal | ABS-CBN News
text: Muling tiniyak ng United States na handa itong sumaklolo sa Pilipinas base sa 1951 Philippine-US Mutual Defense Treaty sakaling hingin ng pagkakataon. Kinondena ng iba pang bansa ang marahas na pagpigil ng China sa rotation and resupply mission ng Philippine Coast Guard sa West Philippine Sea. Nagpa-Patrol, Raffy Santos. TV Patrol, Linggo, 24 Marso 2024

headline: Ilang biyahero umiwas sa dagsa ng pasahero sa Miyerkules Santo | ABS-CBN News
text: Dumami pa ang mga pasahero sa mga paliparan, pantalan at terminal ng bus. Marami kasing natuto na at umiwas sa inaasahang siksikan, lalo na sa Miyerkules Santo. Nagpa-Patrol, Jeffrey Hernaez. TV Patrol, Linggo, 24 Marso 2024

headline: Teves camp pinag-aaralang magreklamo sa UN council | ABS-CBN News
text: Pinag-aaralan ng mga abogado ni dating Negros Oriental congressman Arnolfo Teves Jr. na dumulog sa United Nations dahil umano sa panggigipit ng pamahalaan ng Pilipinas sa kaniyang karapatang pantao. Inireklamo din nila ang pagkuha ng retrato kay Teves sa loob ng kulungan sa Timor-Leste. Sa kabila nito, tiniyak ng National Bureau of Investigation sa dating kongresista ang kaniyang kaligtasan, oras na payagan na siyang ibalik sa Pilipinas. Nagpa-Patrol, Niko Baua. TV Patrol, Linggo, 24 Marso 2024

headline: Filipino Catholics pray for peace in 'conflict areas' on Palm Sunday | ABS-CBN News
text:
Filipino Catholics said they were praying for 'peace for the whole world' on Palm Sunday (March 24) at a mass marking the beginning of the Holy Week.
Hundreds of devotees attended the morning mass at a church in Antipolo City, about 20 kilometers east of Manila, waving their palm fronds as a sign of discipleship and remembrance of Jesus Christ. The blessed palms are later placed outside their houses in a belief that it will ward off evil spirits.
"Almost every day we pray for peace with the world, especially in wars and other conflict areas. Our prayers and sacrifices are all for peace,” said Reynnante Tolentino, the parish rector of the church.
The Holy Week, which begins from Palm Sunday and ends on Easter Sunday, marks the last week of Lent, during which Christians fast, pray and give alms to the needy.
Roman Catholics make up about 80 percent of the more than 110 million Philippine population.
READ: Marcos Jr. reminds public to ‘have meaningful, reflective’ Holy Week
LOOK: Palm Sunday rites open observance of Holy Week
(Production: Jay Ereno, Lorenzo Lesaba, Peter Blaza)

headline: Thousands gather for 'Happy Walk' to mark World Down Syndrome Day | ABS-CBN News
text:
Thousands gather for 21st Happy Walk in celebration of World Down Syndrome Day at the SMX Convention Center, March 24, 2024. Addie Cuadra, ABS-CBN News
MANILA -- Thousands of participants gathered in celebration of World Down Syndrome day at the SMX Convention Center in Pasay City on Sunday. 
Around 4,000 individuals with Down Syndrome, accompanied by their families and supporters, attended the event. Similar festivities took place simultaneously at SM Cebu and SM Bacolod. 
Chad Caluyo, Assistant Vice President of SM Cares, expressed the significance of the event and highlighted its alignment with SM’s commitment to inclusivity and extended gratitude to their partners from the Down Syndrome Association of the Philippines, Inc. (DASPI). Caluyo shared that the event is part of their campaign to be the “mall for all.” 
Antipolo hosts walk-a-thon supporting individuals with Down syndrome
“Itong Happy Walk 2024 ay annual natin na celebration. Kasabay ito ng World Down Syndrome Day, and this activity is a celebration of love and inclusion sa ating SM Supermalls. This is a celebration of the way of embracing and sharing the love of our partners from the Down Syndrome Association of the Philippines, Inc. (DSAPI),” Caluyo said. 
The attendance surpassed their expectations, according to Caluyo, as the turnout exceeded 5,000 participants. He attributed the success not only to DSAPI members but also the broader community, including customers and visitors from nearby provinces.
Caluyo also encouraged everyone to engage in their activities promoting inclusivity: "Iniimbitahan namin ang lahat to participate in all the activities related to inclusivity."
How you can support people with down syndrome

headline: Philippines says 3 soldiers hurt by China water cannon | ABS-CBN News
text:
This frame grab from handout aerial video footage taken and released on March 23, 2024 by the Armed Forces of the Philippines shows a China Coast Guard ship (top) deploying water cannon against the Philippine military-chartered civilian boat Unaizah May 4 during its supply mission near the Second Thomas Shoal in disputed waters of the South China Sea. Armed Forces of the Philippines
Three Philippine troops were injured in the latest China Coast Guard water cannon attack on a Filipino supply vessel near a South China Sea reef, Manila's national security adviser said Sunday.
The Philippine government said Saturday's confrontation caused severe damage to the Unaizah May 4 vessel while it was on its way to deliver troops and provisions to a Philippine navy ship grounded atop the Second Thomas Shoal.
China claims almost the entire South China Sea, brushing off rival claims from other countries, including the Philippines, and an international ruling that its assertion has no legal basis.
National Security Adviser Eduardo Ano said three navy personnel aboard the vessel were injured in Saturday's incident.
The extent and nature of their injuries were not disclosed, though the military said the personnel were treated aboard a coast guard escort ship.

Four crew members were injured by broken glass in an earlier China Coast Guard water cannon attack on the same vessel on March 5.
"This is just an ordinary rotation and resupply or provision operation but look at how the Chinese are reacting," Ano told reporters, according to an interview transcript shared with AFP.
The United States, which has a mutual defence pact with Manila, has denounced the attack.
"The (Chinese) actions are destabilising to the region and show clear disregard for international law," State Department spokesman Matthew Miller said in a statement, reaffirming Washington's mutual defence treaty commitments to Manila.
The China Coast Guard has defended its actions, describing them as "lawful regulation, interception and expulsion" of a foreign vessel that "tried to forcefully intrude" into Chinese waters.
Ano said the damaged vessel has returned to the western province of Palawan after its crew managed to restart its engine.
"We will not be deterred. We will not be intimidated," he said, vowing the Philippines would continue to resupply the garrison on Second Thomas Shoal.
Philippines accuses China of installing floating barriers South China Sea
© Agence France-Presse

headline: PH accuses China of installing floating barriers in South China Sea | ABS-CBN News
text:
China's coast guard said it had taken control measures against Philippine vessels in disputed waters of the South China Sea on Saturday, while the Philippine coast guard decried the moves as "irresponsible and provocative.
The incident occurred in the Second Thomas Shoal and Spratly Islands waters, according to the Chinese Coast Guard. The shoal is home to a small number of Filipino troops stationed on a warship that Manila grounded there in 1999 to reinforce its sovereignty claims.
China Coast Guard used water cannon on resupply boat — AFP
Footage shared by the Armed Forces of the Philippines appeared to show people onboard a rubber boat installing floating barriers at sea, which the Philippines said came from the Chinese Maritime militia to seal off the entrance to the shoal.
China claims almost the entire South China Sea, including the Second Thomas Shoal, which is within the Philippines' 200-mile exclusive economic zone, and has deployed vessels to patrol the disputed atoll. A 2016 ruling by the Permanent Court of Arbitration found that China's sweeping claims have no legal basis.
Can Manila diffuse tensions in the South China Sea?
(Production: Jay Ereno, Peter Blaza)

headline: US condemns China's latest sea attacks vs Philippines | ABS-CBN News
text:
Screen grab from Armed Forces of the Philippines video

MANILA — The United States has condemned China's latest agression in the South China Sea against Philipine vessels and reaffirmed its commitment to the mutual defense pact with Manila.
"The United States stands with its ally the Philippines and condemns the dangerous actions by the People’s Republic of China (PRC) against lawful Philippine maritime operations in the South China Sea on March 23," US State Department spokesperson Matthew Miller said in a statement Saturday.
China Coast Guard used water cannon on resupply boat — AFP
Miller said that the "US-Philippines Mutual Defense Treaty extends to armed attacks on Philippine armed forces, public vessels, or aircraft – including those of its Coast Guard – anywhere in the South China Sea."
On Saturday, Chinese vessels yet again used water cannon against a civilian vessel chartered to resupply Philippine troops on the BRP Sierra Madre, a commissioned Philippine Navy ship on Ayungin Shoal in the West Philippine Sea, the Armed Forces of the Philippines (AFP) said.
The West Philippine Sea is the part of the South China Sea within the Philippines' exclusive economic zone.
In a post on X that included videos of the incident, the AFP said the China Coast Guard directed water cannon at the Unaiza May 4, a ship that had previously been damaged by water cannon while on the same mission.
Miller said China's actions were "destabilizing to the region" and showed "clear disregard for international law."
The Philippines stands by a 2016 the Hague ruling that found that China's sweeping nine-dash line claim over a large part of the South China Sea has no basis.
Why Philippines uses civilian boat for its resupply missions to Ayungin Shoal
"As provided under the 1982 Law of the Sea Convention, the 2016 arbitral decision is final and legally binding on the PRC and the Philippines," Miller said.
"And the United States calls upon the PRC to abide by the ruling and desist from its dangerous and destabilizing conduct."
China in turn accused the Philippine ships of "illegally" intruding into what it says is its sovereign waters. It also accused a Philippine vessel of intentionally ramming its own.
The US, the United Kingdom, the European Union and other security partners have called out previous attempts by Chinese vessels to impede Philippine boats in the West Philippines with aggressive maneuvers and water cannon.

headline: Marcos Jr. reminds public: ‘Have meaningful, reflective’ Holy Week | ABS-CBN News
text:
Devotees flock to Baclaran Church in Parañaque City on Palm Sunday, March 24, 2024. Palm Sunday marks the beginning of Holy Week, when Christians around the world commemorate the Passion of Christ through prayer and contemplation. Mark Demayo, ABS-CBN News
President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. on Palm Sunday urged Filipino Catholics to “have a meaningful and reflective Holy Week” as the lone predominantly Catholic nation in Asia embarks on the final week of Lent.
Holy Week — which starts on Palm Sunday and ends on Easter — is an opportunity to “commemorate the passion, death and resurrection of the Lord Jesus Christ as well as reflect on the many transcendent encounters that beckon us to explore the depths of our souls and rediscover the spirituality that lies at the core of our being,” the President said in his lent message this year.
“This hallowed time of contemplative silence behooves us to ponder on the mysteries that elude our grasp, seek revelation in the face of the inexplicable, and acknowledge the interplay of joy and sorrow in life so that we may understand the path laid before us by Almighty God,” he said.
“In this solemn occasion, let us not only seek to unravel the mysteries of our faith, but also to illuminate the path for others through acts of kindness and selflessness ,” he said.



Palm Sunday, ipinagdiwang ng mga Katoliko
The President said that he also prays for Filipinos to “humbly accept our authentic selves as imperfect beings for it is by becoming truly human that we can experience the divine.”
“Let us always remember to seek the Lord in our desires and to desire Him in our seeking,” he said.
Filipinos are expected to travel to their respective provinces this week as the country starts its long weekend on Maundy Thursday.
DOH on code white alert for Holy Week
LIST: Banking schedules for #SemanaSanta2024
Oil price hike seen for Holy Week 2024

headline: Palm Sunday, ipinagdiwang ng mga Katoliko | ABS-CBN News
text:
Dinagsa ng mga Katoliko ang mga simbahan ngayong Linggo ng Palaspas o Palm Sunday na hudyat na simula ng Semana Santa. 
Sa Quiapo Church sa Maynila, nagdaos ng prusisyon bago ang Misa bandang 4:30 ng madaling-araw na pinangunahan ni Rev. Fr. Rufino Sescon Jr., rector ng simbahan.
Naglakad mula Carriedo Street ang mga dumalo hanggang sa makapasok sa simbahan para sa Misa.
Binabasbasan na rin ng mga pari ang palaspas na bitbit ng mga sumama sa prusisyon.
Ilan sa mga nagsimba si Haidee Labiste at dalawang kaibigan. Galing sa trabaho, dumiretso sila sa simbahan para ipagpatuloy ang tradisyon na makapagsimba sa Linggo ng Palaspas.
“Pinagdadasal ko ang family ko. Good health tsaka maging okay ang trabaho namin, tradisyon na talaga namin ito, na tuwing Palm Sunday mayroon kaming winawagayway na palaspas para next year mayroon din kaming masusunog at mailagay sa noo,” ani Labiste.



Kalusugan din at maayos na pamumuhay ang dasal ni Felicidad Reyes.
“Grabe na ang kahirapan. Ultimong pambili ng ulam, grabe. Halos hindi na makaraos sa hirap,” sabi ni Reyes.
“Sobrang importante [ang Palm Sunday]. Kasama sa buhay na natin ito kung totoo tayong Katoliko,” dagdag niya.
Sa kanyang homily, sinabi ni Fr. Sescon na importante na laging piliin si Hesus araw-araw, sa kabila ng mga tukso.
“Sana punuin natin ng pagmamahal ang mga araw na ito. Marami tayong gagawin, sinimulan natin. Maraming prusisyon sa linggo na ito. May pabasa tayo, Visita Iglesia. Marami tayong gagawin. Magsasakripisyo tayo pero ang lahat ng ito magkakabuluhan lamang, magkakabiyaya lamang kung katulad ni Hesus Nazareno, dadaan tayo sa lagusan ng mga tupa, ang lagusan na may pagmamahal, pagmamalakasakit, pagpapatawad, pagsabi ng tao, at pagpili sa tama,” sabi ni Fr. Sescon.
“Maaari tayo ngayon sa simbahan ay taimtim na taimtim, mukhang banal, mukhang tahimik. Pero paglabas na paglabas natin, naiiba ang ating ugali, naiiba ang ating sinasabi. Para kay Kristo ka ba o laban kay Kristo? Pinipili mo ba si Kristo o iba ang iyong pinipili,” dagdag niya.
DOH on code white alert for Holy Week
PITX expects at least 1.7 million passengers this Holy Week

headline: Fil-Ams protest House resolution passage on PH charter change | ABS-CBN News
text:
Filipino-Americans in New York recently took to the streets to denounce the passage of a House resolution for economic amendments to the Philippine constitution. 
The House of Representatives passed on Wednesday the Resolution of Both Houses 7, which seeks to expand foreign ownership of key local industries. 
House approves proposed changes to 1987 charter
Protestors at the Big Apple condemned the move as they believe it is a threat to Philippine sovereignty. 
They said foreign investments, without proper safeguards, could spell disaster and exploitation of everyday Filipinos. 
"That's a Trojan horse," said Nina Macapulac, secretary general of BAYAN USA. "Foreign investment with no protections for local producers will hurt the regular Filipinos and only benefit the rich."
Anti-charter change protesters also expressed fear that the ultimate goal for amending the constitution is to increase the term limits of those in power. 
"[We] stand firm in declaring that the Marcos-backed push for charter change is a clear threat to democracy," said Sal Vencio of Malaya Movement.
They argue that term limits create a check on power and without it, politicians would be allowed to wield tremendous power and influence.
While the proposed amendment has been sent to the Philippine Senate for approval, the protesters said the amendments are not yet set in stone.
"We still have the arena in the Senate," said Joanne Cesario of Kilusang Mayo Uno. "Many administrations have attempted to amend the constitution but the people have always fought back."
Protesters believe the fight to stop charter change is just beginning. 
They said the Filipino people will ultimately get to decide on amending the charter, if the time comes that proposed amendments are put to a vote in a plebiscite.

headline: Fil-Am legal observers weigh in on Texas immigration law | ABS-CBN News
text:
In the heart of the Lone Star State, Texas, and U.S. lawmakers are engaged in a heated debate over Senate Bill 4.
It’s a sweeping new law in the state that makes it a crime to cross into Texas from a foreign country anywhere other than a legal port of entry, usually the international bridges from Mexico.
The measure empowers state and local police officers to arrest migrants who cross into the state from Mexico without authorization.
On March 19, the U.S. Supreme Court briefly cleared the way for implementation of the law, which the Biden administration has challenged and has argued as unconstitutional.
Under SB4, any migrant seen by the police wading across the Rio Grande could be arrested and charged in state court with a misdemeanor on the first offense.
A second offense is to be considered a felony. Migrants who are arrested could also be ordered during the court process to return to Mexico or face prosecution if they did not agree to go.
Filipino-American Nelvin Adriatico, who ran for state representative in Texas’ District 38, said the move could be detrimental to the ties between authorities and the public.
"There is already a decline of trust," he said. "This is going to make it harder for the police to do their job because it requires the community help to keep the community safe."
Critics said the law is discriminatory and could lead to the detention of innocent people if police officers only have to suspect that they are in the country illegally.
But Texas lawmakers insisted that they devised S.B.4 to closely follow federal law, which already prohibits illegal entry. They also hailed it as crucial for public safety.
Texas has argued that its law was necessary to deter migrants from crossing illegally, which has happened in record numbers over the past year.
There were nearly 250,000 encounters with migrants at the U.S.-Mexico border in December alone – more than any month on record.
Texas Governor Gregg Abbott has also bussed more than 102,000 migrants from Texas to other large cities around the country, such as New York, Chicago, as well as Los Angeles and Washington, D.C.
But the new law still faces an uncertain future. The Supreme Court returned the case to an appellate court to decide whether the law should stand while the challenges made their way through the legal system. That puts the law on hold once again — for the moment.
Filipino immigrants, particularly those without legal documentation, are seen to experience heightened fear and anxiety due to the increased possibility of being stopped by law enforcement and questioned about their immigration status.
With law enforcement officers targeting individuals based on their perceived ethnicity or immigration status – critics worry that Filipinos, especially those who may be mistaken for Latinos, could be unfairly targeted and subjected to scrutiny, even if they are lawful residents or citizens.
Lawyer Jim Narvios advised Fil-Ams in Texas to be ready with their identification cards if asked to pull over when driving. He also cautioned them against providing false information.
"If you are arrested, do not agree to deportation without consulting an attorney," he said. "Other things to consider are making a power of attorney. assigning a guardian for your family legally in the event something happens, and preparing by getting a reputable attorney that can help you in the process."
As SB4 takes effect, Texas finds itself at a crossroads, grappling with questions of immigration enforcement and civil liberties that have implications far beyond its borders.
